metadata.seed: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

patient.query: Yeah, last week I was on my way to class and my girlfriend actually called me up saying that I forgot to take out the trash. And I kind of started thinking, wow, like, I forgot to take out the trash. Something so simple, a simple chore. All I had to do was take out the trash today and I forgot to do it. And then I started to think, well, I can't do something as simple as that. Why is she still with me? Or, and why, why can't I do that? Why can't I do a simple task? Am I good enough for anyone? Am I good enough for her? Kind of starting to feel a little fearful that she would just up and leave me.

neutral: Forgets what was going to do (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like the forgotten trash has sparked worries about your worth and the relationship. What evidence do you have that she might leave because of this? How might you view this situation differently?

patient.query: I don’t know. It just feels like everything's piling up, you know? Like, if I can't even handle something as simple as taking out the trash, how can I expect her to stay? I mean, she’s got every reason to want someone better. It’s hard to shake off those thoughts.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling overwhelmed and worried that your partner might leave because of the small tasks you’re struggling with. What evidence do you have that she would leave if you can’t take out the trash? And what other reasons might she have to stay that you haven’t considered yet?

patient.query: I guess I don’t really have solid evidence. It’s more of a feeling, you know? Sometimes, it just seems like I’m a burden. And, to be honest, I don't think I give her enough credit for how patient she is. But that little voice in my head is so loud, telling me I’m not enough.

neutral: Worried about being a bad mother (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy feeling that you’re a burden and that your inner voice is loud. What moments or thoughts make you think you’re not enough? Are there times when you’ve seen yourself being helpful or supportive that might challenge that voice?

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, there are times when I’ve helped her out or been there for friends, but those moments feel so small compared to all the times I mess things up. It’s like I can’t really let those little victories sink in. I just focus on the mistakes and how I think I’m failing everyone. It’s frustrating because I want to be better, but it feels like I’m stuck.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle of focusing on the mistakes, even when you’ve helped others. What evidence do you have that those moments are insignificant compared to the errors you see? How might you weigh the impact of those small victories differently?

patient.query: I don’t know. I guess when I look back, those moments just feel fleeting. Like, sure, I helped out once, but what about all the times I didn’t show up? Sometimes I wonder if I really made a difference at all. It’s hard to think of those small victories as anything more than just... distractions from what really matters. I just keep going back to feeling like it's not enough.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re wrestling with whether the help you’ve offered truly matters. What moments come to mind where you felt your support made a real difference? How might those experiences shift the way you view your own impact?

patient.query: I mean, there were times when, like, I listened to a friend who was really going through a tough time, and they said it meant a lot to them. But even then, I just brushed it off as, you know, basic stuff. I think I just feel like I should be doing more—something bigger. It’s weird because I want to believe those moments matter, but it’s hard to hold onto that when I’m feeling low. I guess I struggle to see myself in

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a bit torn between wanting to be helpful and feeling that your actions might not be enough. What does “something bigger” look like to you? How do you think that expectation affects how you feel when you’re already low?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess it does feel like I’m stuck between wanting to help and just feeling like whatever I do isn’t really making a difference. "Something bigger" would probably mean being there all the time or having some huge impact, you know? Like, I don’t know, changing someone’s life or being the hero in their story. When I feel low, it just makes everything feel heavier—I start thinking I’m failing at even the little things. Then I spiral, and it’s like

neutral: Fear of being a burden to others (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like when you’re feeling low, you see yourself as failing at even the small things, and that can make everything feel heavier. What evidence do you have that your actions aren’t actually making a difference? Can you think of a time when your help did matter to someone?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: Fear of being a burden to others
retrieved_concepts_used.label: neutral

patient.query: It’s tough to think of those times when things felt good, you know? I guess the moments that really stand out are when I’ve supported someone during a tough time, but it feels so far away now. When I’m down, all I see are the missed calls or the times I couldn’t be there. It’s like those moments overshadow everything else. Sometimes I think I just overthink everything—I mean, maybe they didn’t mean it so much, or maybe they were just being nice

neutral: Working through the pain of grief (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like those supportive moments feel distant, especially when you’re reminded of what you missed. What evidence do you have that those moments are truly far away, versus just hard to recall right now? How might you view those times differently if you considered them as part of a larger pattern of caring and connection? What small ways could you bring those memories into your present experience?

patient.query: It’s tough to think of those times when things felt good. I guess the moments that really stand out are when I’ve supported someone during a tough time, but it feels so far away now. When I’m down, all I see are the missed calls or the times I couldn’t be there. It’s like those moments overshadow everything else. Sometimes I think I just overthink everything. Maybe they didn’t mean it so much, or maybe they were just being nice.

neutral: Working through the pain of grief (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a lot of missed opportunities when you’re feeling low. What evidence do you have that those missed calls or times you couldn’t be there outweigh the moments when you helped others? How might you view those supportive moments differently, or what might a friend say about them? Exploring those alternative views could help you see a more balanced picture.

patient.query: I don’t know, it’s just... it feels like those missed chances stick with me more. I guess if I really think about it, a friend might say that just being there matters, even if it wasn’t all the time. But when I’m lost in my head, I can’t quite see it that way. It’s like my mind takes over, and I focus on what I didn’t do rather than what I did. It just makes everything feel heavier, like I’m drowning

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck on what didn’t happen, and that can weigh heavily on you. What evidence do you have that those missed chances define your worth or your entire experience? Perhaps you can also look for moments where you were present and supportive, even if they weren’t perfect, and see how those moments fit into the bigger picture.
